blocks:
Surah Al-Falaq

Medinan according to most sayings, and it is said to be Meccan The number of its verses is five verses, by consensus (بالإِجماع)* بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ*** (In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful)

(١) قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ الْفَلَقِ ** (1) Say, 'I seek refuge in the Lord of the daybreak,' That which is split open from it (يُفْلَقُ عَنْهُ), meaning that which is separated from it (يُفَرَّقُ عَنْهُ). And it has been specifically designated by custom (عُرْفاً) as the dawn (الصُّبْح), and for that reason, it has been interpreted as such.

In al-Ma'ani, narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): He was asked about الْفَلَقِ . He said: "A fissure (صَدْع) in the Fire (النّار). In it are seventy thousand houses (دَار); in every house, seventy thousand rooms (بَيْت); in every room, seventy thousand black snakes (أَسْوَد); in every black snake, seventy thousand jars (جَرَّة) of poison (سَمّ). The inhabitants of the Fire (أَهْلُ النَّار) must inevitably pass over it."

And al-Qummi said: الْفَلَقِ ** is a pit (جُبّ) in Hell (جَهَنَّم). The inhabitants of the Fire seek refuge from the intensity of its heat. It asked Allah to permit Him to breathe, so He permitted it, and it breathed, and it burned Hell—the narrative (الحديث).

(٢) مِنْ شَرِّ ما خَلَقَ (2) From the evil of what He has created. It was said that the world of creation (عالم الخلق) was specified for seeking refuge from its evil because evil is confined therein, whereas the world of command (عالم الامر) is entirely good. (٣) وَمِنْ شَرِّ غاسِقٍ ** (3) and from the evil of the gloaming, It is the night when its darkness intensifies, like His saying: إِلى غَسَقِ اللَّيْلِ إِذا وَقَبَ ** (until the gloaming of the night) [Q 17:78]. إِذا وَقَبَ ** means when its darkness enters into everything. It was said that the night was specified because harms multiply therein and defense becomes difficult. For this reason, it was said: "The night conceals the woe (الويل)." (٤) وَمِنْ شَرِّ النَّفَّاثاتِ فِي الْعُقَدِ ** (4) and from the evil of the women who blow on the knots, And from the evil of the souls (النفوس) or the sorceress women (النساء السواحر) who tie knots (عقداً) in threads and blow (ينفثن) upon them. Al-Nafth (النّفث) is blowing accompanied by saliva (ريق). (٥) وَمِنْ شَرِّ حاسِدٍ إِذا حَسَدَ ** (5) and from the evil of the envious one when he envies. [This means] when his envy (حسده) becomes manifest and he acts according to its requirement, for before that, its harm does not return to the envied person (المحسود), but rather is confined to the envious person himself due to his distress over the other's joy. In al-Ma'ani, it is narrated marfu‘an (with an elevated chain) that He (the Imam) said regarding this verse: "Have you not seen him when he opens his eyes and looks at you? That is it." It was said that envy (الحسد) was specified for seeking refuge from it because it is the foundation (العمدة) of causing harm (الإضرار).

In al-Kafi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم said: "Envy (الحسد) almost overcomes Divine Decree (القدر)."

In Tibb al-A'immah, narrated from him (Imam al-Sadiq) (peace be upon him): Jibril came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم and said: "O Muhammad!" He said: "I am at Your service, O Jibril!" He said: "Indeed, so-and-so has bewitched you (سحرك) and placed the magic (السحر) in the well of Banu so-and-so. Send to it—meaning the well—the most trustworthy person to you and the greatest in your eyes, who is the equal of your soul (عديل نفسك), so that he may bring you the magic." He said: So the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم sent Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) and said: "Go to the well of Azwan, for therein is magic (سحر) with which Labid ibn A'sam the Jew (اليهودي) bewitched me (اسحرني), and bring it to me." He (Ali) said: "So I set out for the need of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم and descended. The water of the well had become like al-Jana (a type of thick liquid) due to the magic. I searched for it hastily until I reached the bottom of the well (القليب), but I did not find it. Those who were with me said: 'There is nothing in it, so ascend.' I said: 'No, by Allah, I have not lied, nor has He lied—and my soul is not in His Hand like your souls,' meaning the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم. Then I searched gently and extracted a small box (حقّاً). I brought it to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم. He said: 'Open it.' I opened it, and behold, inside the box was a piece of palm spathe (كرب النّخل) containing a bowstring (وتر) upon which were eleven knots (عقدة). Jibril had revealed al-Mu'awwidhatayn (المعوذتين) to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم that day. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم said: 'O Ali, recite them upon the bowstring (الوتر).' So, the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) began to recite an Ayah, and a knot (عقدة) would untie, until he finished them. And Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, removed the magic that had afflicted His Prophet and cured him." In a narration: Jibril and Mika'il came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, and one of them sat on his right and the other on his left. Jibril said to Mika'il: "What is the man's ailment?" Mika'il said: "He is bewitched (مطبوب)." Jibril said: "And who bewitched him?" He said: "Labid ibn A'sam the Jew (اليهودي)." Then the rest of the narrative was mentioned. And from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): He was asked about al-Mu'awwidhatayn (المعوذتين): "Are they part of the Quran?" He said: "Yes, they are part of the Quran." The man said: "They are not part of the Quran in the recitation (قراءة) of Ibn Mas'ud, nor in his Mushaf (مصحفه)." He (peace be upon him) said: "Ibn Mas'ud erred (أخطأ ابن مسعود)," and he said: "Ibn Mas'ud lied (كذب ابن مسعود). They are part of the Quran." The man said: "Should I recite them in the obligatory prayer (المكتوبة)?" He said: "Yes. And do you know the meaning of al-Mu'awwidhatayn and regarding what they were revealed? Indeed, the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم was bewitched by Labid ibn Asim the Jew (اليهودي)." Abu Basir then asked: "And how much did his magic (سحره) almost or possibly affect him?" Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "Yes, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم would imagine that he was having intercourse when he was not, and he would want to reach the door but could not see it until he touched it with his hand. And magic (السحر) is real, but magic is only given power over the eye (العين) and the private parts (الفرج)." So Jibril came to him and informed him of that. He then called Ali (peace be upon him) and sent him to extract that [magic] from the well of Azwan. And the narrative was mentioned. The ‘Ammah (العامّة) (Sunni scholars/community) narrated what is close to that. And al-Qummi narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): The reason for the revelation of al-Mu'awwidhatayn (المعوذتين) was that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم suffered from a fever (وعك), so Jibril descended upon him with these two Surahs and sought refuge for him through them. In al-Majma‘, there is what is close to this. And al-Qummi narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): He was asked that Ibn Mas'ud used to erase al-Mu'awwidhatayn (المعوذتين) from the Mushaf. He said: "My father used to say that Ibn Mas'ud only did that based on his own opinion (برأيه), and they are both part of the Quran." In al-Kafi, narrated from Jabir, he said: Abu Abdillah (Imam al-Sadiq) (peace be upon him) led us in the Maghrib prayer and recited al-Mu'awwidhatayn (المعوذتين), then he said: "They are both part of the Quran." In Thawab al-A'mal and al-Majma‘, narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), he said: "Whoever performs the Witr prayer with al-Mu'awwidhatayn (المعوذتين) and ** قُلْ هُوَ اللَّهُ أَحَدٌ ** (Say: He is Allah, the One), it will be said to him: 'O servant of Allah, rejoice! For Allah has accepted [your deed] and forgiven you.'"
